locked_facts: Gravitational PE: U = -GMm/r (always negative, zero at infinity)
Near Earth's surface: ΔU = mgh (approximation for small h << R)
PE is negative because work must be done against gravity to take mass to infinity
Total energy E = KE + PE; for bound orbit E < 0
Escape: total energy E = 0 (KE = |PE| at surface)
The reference point for U = -GMm/r is at infinity where U = 0
PE depends only on the separation r, not on the path — gravity is conservative
truth_statements: Gravitational potential energy is negative because the reference point is at infinity where U is defined as zero
The negative sign indicates that the system is bound — energy must be added to separate the masses to infinity
Near the surface of Earth the expression U = mgh is a linear approximation of the exact formula -GMm/r
The total mechanical energy of a body in orbit is negative — it is a bound system
For escape velocity the total energy is exactly zero: ½mv² - GMm/R = 0

aha_moment.what_to_show: A PE vs r graph showing U = -GMm/r as a hyperbolic curve. At r = R (surface), U is most negative. As r → ∞, U → 0. An object thrown upward climbs this potential well. If KE at surface exactly equals |U|, it just barely escapes (E = 0).
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: PE well curve with total energy lines: E < 0 (bound), E = 0 (escape), E > 0 (unbound)
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students are confused by negative PE — seeing it as a potential well where 'escape' means climbing to zero makes the sign meaningful

simulation_states.label: PE near surface
simulation_states.description: Object at height h above ground. U = mgh (taking ground as reference). Simple and familiar. But this only works for h << R.
simulation_states.emphasis: mgh is an approximation — works only near the surface

simulation_states.label: PE from infinity
simulation_states.description: Now reference is at infinity (U = 0 there). At Earth's surface, U = -GMm/R (large negative number). PE gets less negative as r increases.
simulation_states.emphasis: PE is always negative — it represents a bound state

simulation_states.label: Escape from PE well
simulation_states.description: PE vs r graph shown as a potential well. A ball at the bottom needs KE = GMm/R to climb out. E = 0 line represents escape. E < 0 lines represent orbits.
simulation_states.emphasis: Escape = climbing out of the gravity well. Total energy = 0 at escape.

simulation_states.label: Interactive
simulation_states.description: Student launches object from surface with adjustable speed. Energy bar shows KE + PE. If total E < 0: object falls back. E = 0: just escapes. E > 0: escapes with speed remaining. PE graph shows position.
simulation_states.emphasis: Increase launch speed — watch total energy cross zero for escape

common_misconceptions.belief: Negative PE means the object has no energy
common_misconceptions.correction: Negative PE means the system is bound — you must add energy to separate the masses. The 'zero' is at infinity. Think of it as a debt: negative PE = energy owed to escape.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show PE well with object at the bottom — it needs energy INPUT to reach zero (escape)

common_misconceptions.belief: U = mgh works at all heights
common_misconceptions.correction: U = mgh is a linear approximation valid only when h << R (Earth's radius). For large heights (satellites, rockets), you must use U = -GMm/r which accounts for the 1/r variation of gravity.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Plot both mgh (straight line) and -GMm/r (curve) — they diverge at large h
